text.title: go-backend Core Rules
text.summary: Ten testable rules covering layout, interface ownership, request DTOs, context, middleware, and worker-pool concurrency.

rule.statement: Use the standard Go layout: cmd/api/main.go for entry point, internal/{handler,service,repository,model,middleware,config} for domain code, pkg/ for public packages, migrations/ for DB migrations.
rule.rationale: internal/ prevents external imports, enforcing encapsulation. cmd/ supports multiple binaries from one module.

rule.statement: Define interfaces in the package that consumes them, not in the package that provides the implementation. service/ defines UserRepository; repository/ provides the implementation.
rule.rationale: This is the Go idiom. Interfaces at the consumer side break import cycles and make mocking straightforward — the test provides a mock that satisfies the interface without importing the real impl.

rule.statement: Use separate *Request structs for HTTP request binding. Never bind directly into domain model structs.
rule.rationale: Domain models may have fields that must never be set by external input (IDs, timestamps, internal flags). DTO separation enforces this boundary.

rule.statement: Keep cmd/api/main.go thin: only wiring (config -> deps -> router -> server). No business logic.
rule.rationale: Agents bloat main.go when not constrained. Thin main makes each layer independently testable.

rule.statement: Pass context.Context as the first argument of every exported function that does I/O. Never capture gin.Context inside a goroutine — derive ctx := c.Request.Context() before spawning.
rule.rationale: Context carries cancellation, deadlines, and trace IDs. Capturing gin.Context in a goroutine is a data race and causes request-context leaks.

rule.statement: Use Gin (or Echo) with small, single-purpose middlewares. Never put business logic in middleware.
rule.rationale: Small middlewares compose cleanly and are easier for agents to understand and extend.

rule.statement: Use ShouldBindJSON (returns error) instead of BindJSON (aborts on error). Use binding struct tags for validation.
rule.rationale: ShouldBind lets handlers return custom error responses; BindJSON writes a 400 and aborts, bypassing the ErrorHandler middleware.

rule.statement: Use a single ErrorHandler middleware that maps AppError to HTTP status. Handlers call c.Error(err) and return — never c.JSON for errors.
rule.rationale: Centralized error mapping ensures consistent error shape and avoids duplicating HTTP status logic in every handler.

rule.statement: Use a Pool struct with a buffered jobs channel and context-aware workers. Pool.Submit must select on both the jobs channel and ctx.Done() to avoid deadlock under cancellation.
rule.rationale: Blocking submit on a full buffer without ctx awareness deadlocks when the caller is cancelled. The select pattern drains cleanly.

rule.statement: Use runtime.NumCPU() as default worker count. Close the jobs channel after all jobs are sent; let workers drain naturally.
rule.rationale: Closing the channel signals workers to exit their range loop cleanly without an explicit stop signal.

rule.statement: Layout matches, interfaces at consumer side, contexts plumbed, ErrorHandler single, Pool selects on ctx.Done.
rule.rationale: Aggregate conclusion target for the decision tree's positive branch.

rule.statement: Service is not HTTP or layout cannot follow the standard — methodology does not apply.
rule.rationale: Aggregate conclusion target for the decision tree's negative branch.
